text: Robotic transabdominal preperitoneal ventral hernia repair. The indications for robotic tap hernia repair include small hernias without wide diastasis recti. These include umbilical hernias as well as the classic difficult hernias such as epigastric, subcostal, and suprapubic hernias where mess fixation is problematic secondary to anatomical constraints.

It is our contention that there is no better protected mesh than mesh protected by the body's own tissue. Peritoneal mesh protection minimizes the risk of visceral adhesion, minimizes the requirement of trans-fascial suture fixation which certainly improves post-operative pain, and maximizes the surface area for mesh incorporation.

While there is literature of conventional laparoscopic preperitoneal ventral hernia repairs, this is a poorly adopted technique in the general surgical community. The robot offers superior vision, precision, and ergonomics, which makes this technique more ideal for public consumption.

Three trocars are placed in the lateral abdomen, including a five millimeter optical trocar for initial intraperitoneal access. The abdominal cavity is insufflated to fifteen millimeters mercury, followed by an initial laparoscopic inspection of the peritoneal cavity.

Under direct vision, a twelve millimeter trocar is placed, followed by the insertion of an eight millimeter robotic trocar in the left lower quadrant. The five millimeter optical trocar is replaced with an eight millimeter robotic trocar.

The surgeon reduces the omentum to expose the hernia defects, revealing two distinct hernias. The hernia on the left is identified as an umbilical hernia, while the other is an incisional hernia from a previous laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon defines the extent of the hernia and incises the peritoneum at least five centimeters proximal to the hernia defect. A preperitoneal flap is developed, resembling the technique used in transabdominal preperitoneal (TAPP) inguinal hernia repairs.
The surgeon approaches the umbilical hernia defect, carefully reducing the preperitoneal fat from the area to expose the hernia.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the peritoneal flap distal to the hernia defect, switching the laparoscopic camera angle from thirty degrees up to thirty degrees down to enhance visualization during the flap dissection.
The surgeon meticulously continues the dissection to avoid disrupting the peritoneum, creating an adequate flap to accommodate the mesh. Following this, the hernia defects are closed.
The surgeon uses an OV-lock suture to close the incisional hernia defect, followed by a second suture to close the umbilical hernia defect.
The surgeon incorporates a bite of subcutaneous skin into the suture repair to recreate the inverted umbilicus, visibly manipulating the tissue to achieve the desired anatomical appearance.
The surgeon re-approximates small disruptions in the peritoneum using III-O Vicryl suture, carefully stitching the tissue to restore continuity.

The assistant guides the Ethicon Physiomesh into the intraperitoneal cavity. The surgeon uses an Ethicon secure strap tacker to attach the mesh securely to the abdominal wall, avoiding transabdominal suture fixation.
The surgeon uses a secure strap stapler to re-approximate the peritoneum, ensuring the tissue layers are aligned and secured.
